question_html: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two dielectric blocks in series. One of the blocks has thickness d1 and dielectric constant k1 and the other has thickness d2 and dielectric constant k2 as shown in Fig. This arrangement can be thought of as a dielectric slab of thickness d (= d1+d2) and effective dielectric constant k. Then k is

options.explanation: Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor filled with dielectric of constant k 1 and thickness d 1 is {tex}C_{1}=\frac{k_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{1}}{/tex} Similarly for other, {tex}C_{2}=\frac{k_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{2}}{/tex} ,having dielectric of constant k 2 and thickness d 2 Both capacitors are in series so equivalent capacitance C is related as: {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}=\frac{d_{1}}{k_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}+\frac{d_{2}}{K_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0} A}\left[\frac{k_{2} d_{1}+k_{1} d_{2}}{k_{1} k_{2}}\right]{/tex} So {tex}C=\frac{k_{1} k_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{k \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} ...(ii) Where d = (d 1 +d 2 ) So, Multiply the numerator and denominator of Eqn. i with (d 1 +d 2 ), {tex}C=\frac{k_{1} k_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)} \cdot \frac{\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}=\frac{k_{1} k_{2}\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(iii) Comparing Eq. II and III, the dielectric constant of the new capacitor is: {tex}k=\frac{k_{1} k_{2}\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)}{/tex}

question_text: Equipotential surfaces are closer in regions of large electric fields compared to regions of lower electric fields. will be more crowded near the sharp edges of a conductor. will be more crowded near regions of large charge densities. will always be equally spaced.
question_html:
Equipotential surfaces

  1. are closer in regions of large electric fields compared to regions of lower electric fields.
  2. will be more crowded near the sharp edges of a conductor.
  3. will be more crowded near regions of large charge densities.
  4. will always be equally spaced.
solution_text: (a), (b) and (c) We know that on any two points of an equipotential surface, a potential difference is zero or of equal potential. {tex}\because E=\frac{-d V}{d r}{/tex} So the electric field intensity is inversely proportional to the separation between equipotential surfaces. So equipotential surfaces are closer in regions of large electric fields. Thus, it verifies the answer (a). The electric field is larger near the sharp edge, due to larger charge density as A is very small {tex}\because \sigma=\frac{q}{A}{/tex} So equipotential surfaces are closer or crowded. It verifies the answer (b). As the electric field {tex}E=\frac{k q}{r^{2}}{/tex} and potential or field decreases as the size of the body increases or vice- versa (case of the earth), so the equipotential surfaces will be more crowded if the charge density {tex}\sigma=\frac{q}{A}{/tex} increases. As the equipotential surface depends on distance r by {tex}E=\frac{-d V}{dr}{/tex} and {tex}V=\frac{k q}{Q}{/tex} Equipotential surface depends on charge density at that place which is different at different places, so equipotential surfaces are not equispaced all over, discarded option (d).
solution_html:
(a), (b) and (c)
We know that on any two points of an equipotential surface, a potential difference is zero or of equal potential.
{tex}\because E=\frac{-d V}{d r}{/tex}
So the electric field intensity is inversely proportional to the separation between equipotential surfaces.
So equipotential surfaces are closer in regions of large electric fields. Thus, it verifies the answer (a).
The electric field is larger near the sharp edge, due to larger charge density as A is very small {tex}\because \sigma=\frac{q}{A}{/tex}
So equipotential surfaces are closer or crowded. It verifies the answer (b).
As the electric field {tex}E=\frac{k q}{r^{2}}{/tex} and potential or field decreases as the size of the body increases or vice- versa (case of the earth), so the equipotential surfaces will be more crowded if the charge density {tex}\sigma=\frac{q}{A}{/tex}  increases.
As the equipotential surface depends on distance r by {tex}E=\frac{-d V}{dr}{/tex} and {tex}V=\frac{k q}{Q}{/tex}
Equipotential surface depends on charge density at that place which is different at different places, so equipotential surfaces are not equispaced all over, discarded option (d).

question_text: In the circuit shown in Fig. initially, key K 1 is closed and key K 2 is open. Then K 1 is opened and K 2 is closed (order is important). [Take Q 1 ′ and Q 2 ′ as charges on C 1 and C 2 and V 1 and V 2 as voltage respectively.] Then charge on C 1 gets redistributed such that V 1 = V 2 charge on C 1 gets redistributed such that Q 1 ′ = Q 2 ′ charge on C 1 gets redistributed such that C 1 V 1 + C 2 V 2 = C 1 E charge on C 1 gets redistributed such that Q 1 ′ + Q 2 ′ = Q
question_html:
In the circuit shown in Fig. initially, key K1 is closed and key K2 is open. Then K1 is opened and K2 is closed (order is important). [Take Q1′ and Q2′ as charges on C1 and C2 and V1 and V2 as voltage respectively.]
Then 

  1. charge on C1 gets redistributed such that V1 = V2
  2. charge on C1 gets redistributed such that Q1′ = Q2′
  3. charge on C1 gets redistributed such that C1V1 + C2V2 = C1E
  4. charge on C1 gets redistributed such that Q1′ + Q2′ = Q
solution_text: When K 1 is closed keeping K 2 open, the capacitor C 1 gets charged by a battery of potential E. Now when k 1 opens, C 1 remains charged. When K 2 closes keeping K 1 open, C 2 gets charged by redistribution of charge of C 1 between C 1 and C 2 . Let charge on C 1 , which is charged by a battery, was Q then after redistribution of charge Q = Q 1 + Q 2 by the law of conservation of charge. So answer (d) is verified. As C 1 and C 2 both are in parallel combination, so their potential will be equal, i.e., V 1 = V 2 . It verifies the answer (a).
solution_html: When K1 is closed keeping K2 open, the capacitor C1 gets charged by a battery of potential E. Now when k1 opens, C1 remains charged. When K2 closes keeping K1 open, C2 gets charged by redistribution of charge of C1 between C1 and C2. Let charge on C1, which is charged by a battery, was Q then after redistribution of charge Q = Q1 + Q2 by the law of conservation of charge. So answer (d) is verified. As C1 and C2 both are in parallel combination, so their potential will be equal, i.e., V1 = V2. It verifies the answer (a).

In A : V remains same and hence Q changes. In B : Q remains same and hence V changes.
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is connected to a battery as shown in Fig. Consider two situations:

A: Key K is kept closed and plates of capacitors are moved apart using the insulating handle.
B: Key K is opened and plates of capacitors are moved apart using the insulating handle.
Choose the correct option(s).

  1. In A : Q remains same but C changes.
  2. In B : V remains same but C changes.
  3. In A : V remains same and hence Q changes.
  4. In B : Q remains same and hence V changes.
solution_text: In situation A: when the space between the plates of a capacitor increases, the capacitance decreases by relation {tex}C=\frac{K \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} , but battery remains the same, .i.e., the potential difference across plate remains ‘V’ same. So by Q = CV relation, Q also decreases verifies answer (c) and discards answer (a). Now for situation B:-When key K is opened and plates of capacitors are moved apart using insulating handle. The charge stored by isolated charged capacitor remains conserved. The separation between two plates is increasing which in turn decreases its capacitance with the decrease of capacitance, potential difference V increases as V=Q/C
solution_html:
1. In situation A: when the space between the plates of a capacitor increases, the capacitance decreases by relation {tex}C=\frac{K \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex}, but battery remains the same, .i.e., the potential difference across plate remains ‘V’ same. So by Q = CV relation, Q also decreases verifies answer (c) and discards answer (a).
  2. Now for situation B:-When key K is opened and plates of capacitors are moved apart using insulating handle.
    The charge stored by isolated charged capacitor remains conserved. The separation between two plates is increasing which in turn decreases its capacitance with the decrease of capacitance, potential difference V increases as V=Q/C

question_text: Consider two conducting spheres of radii R 1 and R 2 with R 1 > R 2 . If the two are at the same potential, the larger sphere has more charge than the smaller sphere. State whether the charge density of the smaller sphere is more or less than that of the largest one.
question_html: Consider two conducting spheres of radii R1 and R2 with R1 > R2. If the two are at the same potential, the larger sphere has more charge than the smaller sphere. State whether the charge density of the smaller sphere is more or less than that of the largest one.
solution_text: Let Q 1 and Q 2 be the charges on radii R 1 and R 2 we know that {tex}V_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \frac{Q_{1}}{R_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}V_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \frac{Q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex} As V 1 = V 2 , so: {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in} \frac{Q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in} \frac{Q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{Q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{Q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{1}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1} \times 4 \pi\left(R_{1}\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{2} \times 4 \pi\left(R_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}{/tex} Since R 1 > R 2 it follows that {tex}\sigma_{1}<\sigma_{2}{/tex} i.e. the charge density of smaller sphere is less than that of larger sphere.
solution_html:
Let Q1 and Q2 be the charges on radii R1 and R2 we know that
{tex}V_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \frac{Q_{1}}{R_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}V_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \frac{Q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex}
As V1 = V2, so:
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in} \frac{Q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in} \frac{Q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{Q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{Q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{1}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1} \times 4 \pi\left(R_{1}\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{2} \times 4 \pi\left(R_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}{/tex}
Since R1 > R2 it follows that
{tex}\sigma_{1}<\sigma_{2}{/tex}
i.e. the charge density of smaller sphere is less than that of larger sphere.

question_text: Can there be a potential difference between two adjacent conductors carrying the same charge?
question_html: Can there be a potential difference between two adjacent conductors carrying the same charge?
solution_text: If in two conductors flowing current is the same then both may be considered in series. So Ohm’s law becomes V {tex}\propto{/tex} R. i.e. if the resistances (which depend on {tex}\rho{/tex} , l and A) are different then the potential differences will be different. So there can be a potential differences between two adjacent conductors carrying the same charge or current if either their length or area of cross-section (A) and resistivity are different.
solution_html:
If in two conductors flowing current is the same then both may be considered in series. So Ohm’s law becomes V {tex}\propto{/tex} R. i.e. if the resistances (which depend on {tex}\rho{/tex}, l and A) are different then the potential differences will be different.
So there can be a potential differences between two adjacent conductors carrying the same charge or current if either their length or area of cross-section (A) and resistivity are different.

question_text: Prove that a closed equipotential surface with no charge within itself must enclose an equipotential volume.
question_html: Prove that a closed equipotential surface with no charge within itself must enclose an equipotential volume.
solution_text: Let us consider that inside the enclosed equipotential surface, potential is not same. Let the potential just inside the equipotential surface is different to that on the equipotential surface, causing in a potential gradient {tex}\frac{d V}{d r}=E{/tex} . So electric field will exist inside surface which is equal to {tex}E = \frac{d V}{d r}{/tex} . The field lines pointing inward or outward from the surface are perpendicular to equipotential surfaces or the field lines cannot be on the equipotential surface. The field lines can be on the equipotential surface if field lines can originate from the charge inside, which contradicts the original assumption. Hence, the entire volume inside equipotential surface has no charge.

question_text: A capacitor has some dielectric between its plates, and the capacitor is connected to a DC source. The battery is now disconnected and then the dielectric is removed. State whether the capacitance, the energy stored in it, electric field, the charge stored and the voltage will increase, decrease, or remain constant.
question_html: A capacitor has some dielectric between its plates, and the capacitor is connected to a DC source. The battery is now disconnected and then the dielectric is removed. State whether the capacitance, the energy stored in it, electric field, the charge stored and the voltage will increase, decrease, or remain constant.
solution_text: {tex}\because{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{k \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} As k is positive and more than one, so by removing the dielectric slab and keeping A and d constant, the capacitance of the capacitor will decrease. When the battery and dielectric slab from the capacitor is removed, the charge remains the same as it was when the battery connected earlier. As the energy stored in the capacitor is {tex}\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} . When capacitance C is decreased by removing the dielectric slab but q remains the same, so the energy stored in the capacitor will increase. We know that {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}{/tex} , where q is same and C is decreased so potential will increase. The potential will increase. As {tex}E=\frac{V}{d}{/tex} , the distance between plates of the capacitor is the same and potential is increased as discussed above, so the electric field between the plates of the capacitor will increase.

question_text: Find the equation of the equipotentials for an infinite cylinder of radius r 0 , carrying charge of linear density {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
question_html: Find the equation of the equipotentials for an infinite cylinder of radius r0, carrying charge of linear density {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
solution_text: Consider a Gaussian cylindrical dotted surface, S at a distance r from the centre of the cylinder of radius r0 of infinite length. The electric field lines are radial and perpendicular to the surface. Let electric field intensity on Gaussian surface at P is E, and total charge q on cylinder will be q = {tex}\lambda l{/tex} So, by Gauss's law, {tex}\oint_{S} E.d s=\frac{\lambda l}{\varepsilon_{0}} \Rightarrow\left[E_{r} \cos \theta\right]_{0}^{2 \pi r}=\frac{\lambda l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E r (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl) = {tex}\frac{\lambda l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E_{r}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi r \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} We know that electric field Er at distance r from centre of cylinder {tex}E_{r}=\frac{-d V}{d r}{/tex} So potential difference at distance r 0 and r from the centre of cylinder is given by: dV = -Er.dr [ {tex}\because E=\frac{-d V}{d r}{/tex} ] V(r) - V(r 0 ) = {tex}-\int E_{r} d r{/tex} = {tex}=\int_{r_{0}}^{r} \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{\rho} r} \cdot d r=\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \int_{r_{0}}^{r} \frac{d r}{r}=\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{\varepsilon} r\right]_{0}^{r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{\varepsilon} r-\log _{\varepsilon} r_{0}\right]=\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{\varepsilon} r\right]_{r_{0}}^{r}{/tex} = {tex}\log _{e} \frac{r}{r_{0}}=\frac{-2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\lambda}\left[V(r)-V\left(r_{0}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{r_{0}}=e^{\frac{-2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\lambda}\left[V(r)-V\left(r_{0}\right)\right]}{/tex} {tex}r=r_{0} e^{\frac{-2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\lambda}\left[V(r)-v\left(r_{0}\right)\right]}{/tex} So equipotential surfaces are the coaxial curved surface of cylinders with a given cylinder of radius r related as above.
solution_html:
Consider a Gaussian cylindrical dotted surface, S at a distance r from the centre of the cylinder of radius r0 of infinite length.
The electric field lines are radial and perpendicular to the surface.

Let electric field intensity on Gaussian surface at P is E, and total charge q on cylinder will be q = {tex}\lambda l{/tex}
So, by Gauss's law,
{tex}\oint_{S} E.d s=\frac{\lambda l}{\varepsilon_{0}} \Rightarrow\left[E_{r} \cos \theta\right]_{0}^{2 \pi r}=\frac{\lambda l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Er(2{tex}\pi{/tex}rl) = {tex}\frac{\lambda l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} 
{tex}E_{r}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi r \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
We know that electric field Er at distance r from centre of cylinder {tex}E_{r}=\frac{-d V}{d r}{/tex}
So potential difference at distance r0 and r from the centre of cylinder is given by:
dV = -Er.dr [{tex}\because E=\frac{-d V}{d r}{/tex}]
V(r) - V(r0) = {tex}-\int E_{r} d r{/tex}
= {tex}=\int_{r_{0}}^{r} \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{\rho} r} \cdot d r=\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \int_{r_{0}}^{r} \frac{d r}{r}=\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{\varepsilon} r\right]_{0}^{r}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{\varepsilon} r-\log _{\varepsilon} r_{0}\right]=\frac{-\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{\varepsilon} r\right]_{r_{0}}^{r}{/tex}
= {tex}\log _{e} \frac{r}{r_{0}}=\frac{-2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\lambda}\left[V(r)-V\left(r_{0}\right)\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{r}{r_{0}}=e^{\frac{-2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\lambda}\left[V(r)-V\left(r_{0}\right)\right]}{/tex}
{tex}r=r_{0} e^{\frac{-2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\lambda}\left[V(r)-v\left(r_{0}\right)\right]}{/tex}
So equipotential surfaces are the coaxial curved surface of cylinders with a given cylinder of radius r related as above.

question_text: Two-point charges of magnitude +q and -q are placed at (- {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} , 0, 0) and ( {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} , 0, 0) respectively. Find the equation of the equipotential surface where the potential is zero.
question_html: Two-point charges of magnitude +q and -q are placed at (-{tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex}, 0, 0) and ({tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex}, 0, 0) respectively. Find the equation of the equipotential surface where the potential is zero.
solution_text: The potential due to charge +q and -q will be zero in between the line joining the two charges +q and -q. Let zero potential is at S. Then equipotential surface will pass through S and perpendicular to line joining two chargers or AB. {tex}r_{1}^{2}=A S^{2}+S P^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}r_{1}=\sqrt{\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}r_{2}=\sqrt{\left(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} Since the net potential at P = O, we have {tex}\frac{k q}{r_{1}}+\frac{k(-q)}{r_{2}}=0{/tex} where k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}k q\left[\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right]=0{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} kq {tex}\ne{/tex} 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}=\left(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}+\frac{d^{2}}{4}+d x=x^{2}+\frac{d^{2}}{4}-d x{/tex} 2dx = 0 2d {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0 So equipotential surface will be perpendicular to X-axis passing through x = 0 i.e., origin in Y-Z plane.
solution_html:
The potential due to charge +q and -q will be zero in between the line joining the two charges +q and -q. Let zero potential is at S.

Then equipotential surface will pass through S and perpendicular to line joining two chargers or AB.
 {tex}r_{1}^{2}=A S^{2}+S P^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}{/tex}
{tex}r_{1}=\sqrt{\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}r_{2}=\sqrt{\left(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
Since the net potential at P = O, we have 
{tex}\frac{k q}{r_{1}}+\frac{k(-q)}{r_{2}}=0{/tex} where k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}k q\left[\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right]=0{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} kq {tex}\ne{/tex} 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}=\left(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(x+\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}+\frac{d^{2}}{4}+d x=x^{2}+\frac{d^{2}}{4}-d x{/tex}
2dx = 0
2d {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0
So equipotential surface will be perpendicular to X-axis passing through x = 0 i.e., origin in Y-Z plane.

question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is filled by a dielectric whose relative permittivity varies with the applied voltage (U ) as {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex} U where {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2V -1 . A similar capacitor with no dielectric is charged to U 0 = 78 V. It is then connected to the uncharged capacitor with the dielectric. Find the final voltage on the capacitors.
question_html: A parallel plate capacitor is filled by a dielectric whose relative permittivity varies with the applied voltage (U ) as {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex}U where {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2V-1. A similar capacitor with no dielectric is charged to U0 = 78 V. It is then connected to the uncharged capacitor with the dielectric. Find the final voltage on the capacitors.
solution_text: Let C be the capacitance of capacitor C 1 without dielectric then charge q 1 = CU where U is the final potential of C 1 when connected to C 2 the capacitor filled with dielectric {tex}\in_0{/tex} C 2 = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} C q 2 = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} CU = {tex}\alpha{/tex} UCU = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} CU 2 Initial charge q 0 of C 1 when charged at potential of U 0 = 78 V is q 0 = CU 0 = 78 C By the law of conservation of charge q 0 = q 1 + q 2 78C = CU + {tex}\alpha{/tex} CU 2 78 = U + {tex}\alpha{/tex} U 2 [ {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2 per volt] {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = U + 2U 2 or 2U 2 + U - 78 = 0 U = {tex}\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1-4 \cdot 2(-78)}}{2.2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1+624}}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{625}}{4}=\frac{-1 \pm 25}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1+25}{4}{/tex} as U is positive = {tex}\frac{24}{4}=6{/tex} volts Final potential on both the capacitors becomes 6 volts.
solution_html:
Let C be the capacitance of capacitor C1 without dielectric then charge q1 = CU where U is the final potential of C1 when connected to C2 the capacitor filled with dielectric {tex}\in_0{/tex} 
C2 = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}C
q2 = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}CU
= {tex}\alpha{/tex}UCU
= {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} CU2 
Initial charge q0 of C1 when charged at potential of U0 = 78 V is
q0 = CU0 = 78 C
By the law of conservation of charge
q0 = q1 + q2
78C = CU + {tex}\alpha{/tex}CU2 
78 = U + {tex}\alpha{/tex}U2 [{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2 per volt]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = U + 2U2 
or 2U2 + U - 78 = 0
U = {tex}\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1-4 \cdot 2(-78)}}{2.2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1+624}}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{625}}{4}=\frac{-1 \pm 25}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1+25}{4}{/tex} as U is positive
= {tex}\frac{24}{4}=6{/tex} volts
Final potential on both the capacitors becomes 6 volts.

question_text: A capacitor is made of two circular plates of radius R each, separated by a distance d << R. The capacitor is connected to a constant voltage. A thin conducting disc of radius r << R and thickness t << r is placed at a center of the bottom plate. Find the minimum voltage required to lift the disc if the mass of the disc is m.
question_html: A capacitor is made of two circular plates of radius R each, separated by a distance d << R. The capacitor is connected to a constant voltage. A thin conducting disc of radius r << R and thickness t << r is placed at a center of the bottom plate. Find the minimum voltage required to lift the disc if the mass of the disc is m.
solution_text: Let A and B are circular plates of radius R separated by distance d << R kept horizontally. A thin conducting disc D of radius r << R of thickness ‘t’ is placed concentrically on lower plate B as shown in figure. Let plate A and B charged with potential V. The magnitude of electric field E between plates of capacitor {tex}E=\frac{V}{d}\left[\because E=\frac{-d V}{d r}\right]{/tex} Consider Gaussian surface along circular disc D. By Gauss's law, {tex}\oint E \cdot d s=\frac{q^{\prime}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{d} \pi r^{2}=\frac{q^{\prime}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} q’ is the charge conducted by plate B to disc D during charging. Nature of charge on plate B and disc will be same so repulsive force acts between B and D. So, the charge on disc D = q' = {tex}\frac{V}{d} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} Electrostatic repulsive force acting on disc in upward direction {tex}F=q^{\prime} E=\frac{V}{d} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0} \cdot \frac{V}{d}=\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} This repulsive force will be balanced by weight mg of disc D. mg = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}V^{2}=\frac{m g d^{2}}{\pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} So minimum voltage V to lift the disc {tex}V=\sqrt{\frac{m g d^{2}}{\pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A and B are circular plates of radius R separated by distance d << R kept horizontally.

A thin conducting disc D of radius r << R of thickness ‘t’ is placed concentrically on lower plate B as shown in figure.
Let plate A and B charged with potential V.
The magnitude of electric field E between plates of capacitor
{tex}E=\frac{V}{d}\left[\because E=\frac{-d V}{d r}\right]{/tex}
Consider Gaussian surface along circular disc D.
By Gauss's law, {tex}\oint E \cdot d s=\frac{q^{\prime}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{V}{d} \pi r^{2}=\frac{q^{\prime}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
q’ is the charge conducted by plate B to disc D during charging. Nature of charge on plate B and disc will be same so repulsive force acts between B and D.
So, the charge on disc D = q' = {tex}\frac{V}{d} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}
Electrostatic repulsive force acting on disc in upward direction
{tex}F=q^{\prime} E=\frac{V}{d} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0} \cdot \frac{V}{d}=\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}
This repulsive force will be balanced by weight mg of disc D.
mg = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}} \pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}
{tex}V^{2}=\frac{m g d^{2}}{\pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
So minimum voltage V to lift the disc
{tex}V=\sqrt{\frac{m g d^{2}}{\pi r^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex}

question_text: In a quark model of elementary particles, a neutron is made of one up quarks [charge ( {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ) e] and two down quarks [charges –( {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} ) e]. Assume that they have a triangle configuration with side length of the order of 10 –15 m. Calculate the electrostatic potential energy of neutron and compare it with its mass 939 MeV. Repeat above exercise for a proton which is made of two up and one down quark.
question_html:
1. In a quark model of elementary particles, a neutron is made of one up quarks [charge ({tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}) e] and two down quarks [charges –({tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}) e]. Assume that they have a triangle configuration with side length of the order of 10–15 m. Calculate the electrostatic potential energy of neutron and compare it with its mass 939 MeV.
  2. Repeat above exercise for a proton which is made of two up and one down quark.
solution_text: {tex}q_{d}=-\frac{1}{3} e{/tex} [charge on down quark] {tex}q_{u}=+\frac{2}{3} e{/tex} [charge on up quark] Potential energy for charges is given by {tex}U=\frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r}+\frac{k q_{1} q_{3}}{r}+\frac{k q_{2} q_{3}}{r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U_{n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(-q_{d}\right)\left(-q_{d}\right)}{r}+\frac{\left(-q_{d}\right) q_{u}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{q_{u}\left(-q_{d}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q_{d}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[+q_{d}-q_{u}-q_{u}\right]{/tex} [Talking sign of charge] {tex}=\frac{q d}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[q_{d}-2 q_{u}\right]=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{1}{3} e}{10^{-15}}\left[\frac{1}{3} e-2 \cdot \frac{2}{3} e\right]{/tex} [nature sign of charges taken already] {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times e}{3 \times 10^{-15}} \cdot \frac{e}{3}[1-4]{/tex} Joule {tex}=\frac{-3 \times 9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-15}}{9 \times 10^{-15}}{/tex} Joule = -7.68 x10 -14 J {tex}-7.68 X10 ^{-14}\over 1.6X10^{-19}{/tex} = -4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14+19 e V = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 e V = -0.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 e V U = -0.48 MeV So, charges inside neutron [1q u and 2q d ] are attracted by the energy of 0.48 MeV. The energy released by a neutron when converted into energy is 939 MeV. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required ratio {tex}=\frac{1-0.481 \mathrm{MeV}}{939 \mathrm{MeV}}{/tex} = 0.0005111 = 5.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 P.E. Of proton consists of 2 up and 1 down quark r = 10 -15 m {tex}q_{d}=-\frac{1}{3} e, q_{u}=\frac{2}{3} e{/tex} {tex}U_{p}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{u} \times q_{u}}{r}+\frac{q_{u}\left(-q_{d}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}+\frac{q_{u}\left(-q_{d}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q_{u}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[q_{u}-q_{d}-q_{d}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q_{u}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[q_{u}-2 q_{d}\right]=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{10^{-15}} \frac{2}{3} e\left[\frac{2}{3} e-2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} e\right]=0{/tex} potential energy is zero for this case.
solution_html:
1. {tex}q_{d}=-\frac{1}{3} e{/tex} [charge on down quark]
    {tex}q_{u}=+\frac{2}{3} e{/tex} [charge on up quark]
    Potential energy for charges  is given by  {tex}U=\frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}U=\frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r}+\frac{k q_{1} q_{3}}{r}+\frac{k q_{2} q_{3}}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore U_{n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(-q_{d}\right)\left(-q_{d}\right)}{r}+\frac{\left(-q_{d}\right) q_{u}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{q_{u}\left(-q_{d}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q_{d}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[+q_{d}-q_{u}-q_{u}\right]{/tex} [Talking sign of charge]
    {tex}=\frac{q d}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[q_{d}-2 q_{u}\right]=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{1}{3} e}{10^{-15}}\left[\frac{1}{3} e-2 \cdot \frac{2}{3} e\right]{/tex}
    [nature sign of charges taken already]
    {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times e}{3 \times 10^{-15}} \cdot \frac{e}{3}[1-4]{/tex} Joule
    {tex}=\frac{-3 \times 9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-15}}{9 \times 10^{-15}}{/tex}  Joule = -7.68 x10-14 J
    {tex}-7.68 X10 ^{-14}\over 1.6X10^{-19}{/tex}= -4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14+19 e V = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 e V = -0.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 e V
    U = -0.48 MeV
    So, charges inside neutron [1qu and 2qd] are attracted by the energy of 0.48 MeV.
    The energy released by a neutron when converted into energy is 939 MeV.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required ratio {tex}=\frac{1-0.481 \mathrm{MeV}}{939 \mathrm{MeV}}{/tex} = 0.0005111 = 5.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
  2. P.E. Of proton consists of 2 up and 1 down quark
    r = 10-15 m
    {tex}q_{d}=-\frac{1}{3} e, q_{u}=\frac{2}{3} e{/tex}
    {tex}U_{p}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{u} \times q_{u}}{r}+\frac{q_{u}\left(-q_{d}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}+\frac{q_{u}\left(-q_{d}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q_{u}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[q_{u}-q_{d}-q_{d}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q_{u}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\left[q_{u}-2 q_{d}\right]=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{10^{-15}} \frac{2}{3} e\left[\frac{2}{3} e-2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} e\right]=0{/tex}  potential energy is zero for this case.

question_text: Two metal spheres, one of radius R and the other of radius 2R, both have the same surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} . They are brought in contact and separated. What will be new surface charge densities on them?
question_html: Two metal spheres, one of radius R and the other of radius 2R, both have the same surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}. They are brought in contact and separated. What will be new surface charge densities on them?
solution_text: Let surface charge density of both the spheres are {tex}\sigma{/tex} and their charges are q 1 and q 2 . {tex}\therefore q_{1}=\sigma \cdot A_{1}=\sigma .4 \pi R^{2}{/tex} {tex}q_{2}=\sigma \cdot A_{2}=\sigma \cdot 4 \pi(2 R)^{2}=\sigma \cdot 4 \pi R^{2} \cdot 4=4 q_{1}{/tex} Both charged spheres are kept in contact, so charge flows between them and their potential becomes equal, let the charges on them now become q 1' and q 2' . So, V 1 = V 2 ( {tex}\because V=\frac{k q}{r}{/tex} ) So, {tex}\frac{k q_{1}}{R}=\frac{k q_{2}}{(2 R)}\left[\because k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex} Where q 1' and q 2' are the charges on spheres after redistribution of charges {tex}\frac{q_{1}^{'}}{R}=\frac{q_{2}^{'}}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}q_{2}^{\prime}=2 q_{1}^{'}{/tex} .....(i) By law of conservation of charges q 1 + q 2 = {tex}q_{2}^{\prime} +2 q_{1}^{'}{/tex} q 1 + 4q 1 = {tex}q_{2}^{\prime} +2 q_{1}^{'}{/tex} (from (i)) 5q 1 = {tex}3 q_{1}^{'}{/tex} {tex}q_{1}^{'}=\frac{20}{3} \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex} {tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}^{'}}{A_{1}}=\frac{\frac{20}{3} \pi R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\frac{5}{3} \sigma{/tex} {tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}^{'}}{A_{2}}=\frac{2 \cdot q_{1}^{'}}{4 \cdot \pi(2 R)^{2}}=\frac{2 \cdot \frac{20}{3} \pi R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi \cdot 4 R^{2}}=\frac{5}{6} \sigma{/tex} Hence, {tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{5}{3} \sigma{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{5}{6} \sigma{/tex}
solution_html:
Let surface charge density of both the spheres are {tex}\sigma{/tex} and their charges are q1 and q2.
{tex}\therefore q_{1}=\sigma \cdot A_{1}=\sigma .4 \pi R^{2}{/tex}
{tex}q_{2}=\sigma \cdot A_{2}=\sigma \cdot 4 \pi(2 R)^{2}=\sigma \cdot 4 \pi R^{2} \cdot 4=4 q_{1}{/tex}
Both charged spheres are kept in contact, so charge flows between them and their potential becomes equal, let the charges on them now become q1' and q2'.
So, V1 = V2  ({tex}\because V=\frac{k q}{r}{/tex})
So, {tex}\frac{k q_{1}}{R}=\frac{k q_{2}}{(2 R)}\left[\because k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}
Where q1' and q2'  are the charges on spheres after redistribution of charges
{tex}\frac{q_{1}^{'}}{R}=\frac{q_{2}^{'}}{2 R}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}q_{2}^{\prime}=2 q_{1}^{'}{/tex}   .....(i)
By law of conservation of charges
q1 + q2 = {tex}q_{2}^{\prime} +2 q_{1}^{'}{/tex}
q1 + 4q1 = {tex}q_{2}^{\prime} +2 q_{1}^{'}{/tex} (from (i))
5q1 = {tex}3 q_{1}^{'}{/tex}
{tex}q_{1}^{'}=\frac{20}{3} \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex}
{tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}^{'}}{A_{1}}=\frac{\frac{20}{3} \pi R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\frac{5}{3} \sigma{/tex}
{tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}^{'}}{A_{2}}=\frac{2 \cdot q_{1}^{'}}{4 \cdot \pi(2 R)^{2}}=\frac{2 \cdot \frac{20}{3} \pi R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi \cdot 4 R^{2}}=\frac{5}{6} \sigma{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{5}{3} \sigma{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{5}{6} \sigma{/tex}

question_text: In the circuit shown in Fig, initially, K 1 is closed and K 2 is open. What are the charges on each capacitor? Then K 1 was opened and K 2 was closed (order is important), What will be the charge on each capacitor now? [C = 1µF]
question_html: In the circuit shown in Fig, initially, K1 is closed and K2 is open. What are the charges on each capacitor? Then K1 was opened and K2 was closed (order is important), What will be the charge on each capacitor now? [C = 1µF]
solution_text: When K 2 is open and K 1 is closed the capacitors C 1 and C 2 will charge and potentially develops across them i.e., V 1 and V 2 respectively which will be equal to the potential of battery 9 V. In the circuit, when initially K1 is closed and K2 is open, the capacitors C1 and C2 connected in series with battery acquire equal charge. {tex}\therefore{/tex} V 1 + V 2 = 9 ...(i) {tex}\because V=\frac{q}{C}=o r V \propto \frac{1}{C}{/tex} {tex}\text { Or } \frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{C_{2}}{C_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{3 C}{6 C}{/tex} 3V 2 = 6V 1 V 2 = 2V 1 ...(ii) From Eqns. i and ii V 1 + 2V 1 = 9 3V 1 = 9 V 1 = 3 Volt V 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Volt = 6 Volt {tex}\therefore{/tex} q 1 = C 1 V 1 = 6C {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 18C[(from ii C = 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F) =18 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} C q 2 = C 2 V 2 = 3C {tex}\times{/tex} 6 =3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} C So, charges on each capacitor i.e., q 1 = q 2 = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} C When k 1 is open and k 2 is closed then charge q 2 will be distributed among C 2 and C 3 . Let it be q 2 and q 3 . {tex}\therefore{/tex} q 2 = q 2 + q 3 As C 2 and C 3 are now in parallel combination so their potentials remain same (V) {tex}\therefore{/tex} q 2 = C 2 V+ C 3 V {tex}18 \mu \mathrm{C}=3 \times 1 \mu \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{V}+3 \times 1 / \mu \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{V}{/tex} 18 = 6V V = 3Volt. So potential on C 2 and C 3 capacitors are 3 Volt each q 2 = C 2 V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Volt = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} C q 3 = C 3 V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Volt = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution_html:
When K2 is open and K1 is closed the capacitors C1 and C2 will charge and potentially develops across them i.e., V1 and V2 respectively which will be equal to the potential of battery 9 V. In the circuit, when initially K1 is closed and K2 is open, the capacitors C1 and C2 connected in series with battery acquire equal charge.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V1 + V2 = 9 ...(i)
{tex}\because V=\frac{q}{C}=o r V \propto \frac{1}{C}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Or } \frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{C_{2}}{C_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{3 C}{6 C}{/tex}
3V2 = 6V1
V2 = 2V1 ...(ii)
From Eqns. i and ii
V1 + 2V1 = 9
3V1 = 9
V1 = 3 Volt
V2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Volt = 6 Volt
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q1 = C1V1 = 6C {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 18C[(from ii C = 1{tex}\mu{/tex}F)
=18 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
q2 = C2V2 = 3C {tex}\times{/tex} 6
=3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex}F {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 18{tex}\mu{/tex} C
So, charges on each capacitor i.e., q1 = q2 = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex}C
When k1 is open and k2 is closed then charge q2 will be distributed among C2 and C3. Let it be q2 and q3.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  q2 = q2 + q3
As C2 and C3 are now in parallel combination so their potentials remain same (V)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  q2 = C2 V+ C3 V
{tex}18 \mu \mathrm{C}=3 \times 1 \mu \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{V}+3 \times 1 / \mu \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{V}{/tex}
18 = 6V
V = 3Volt.
So potential on C2 and C3 capacitors are 3 Volt each
q2 = C2 V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Volt = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
q3 = C3 V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Volt = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} C

question_text: Calculate potential on the axis of a disc of radius R due to a charge Q uniformly distributed on its surface.
question_html: Calculate potential on the axis of a disc of radius R due to a charge Q uniformly distributed on its surface.
solution_text: Consider a point P on the axis perpendicular to the plane of disc and at distance x from the center O of the disc as shown in the figure. Now consider a ring of radius r of thickness dr on a disc of radius R, as shown in the figure, Let us consider disc is divided into a large number of rings, Again let the charge on the ring is dq then potential dV due to ring at P, will be {tex}d V=\frac{k d q}{r^{\prime}}\left[\because r^{\prime}=\sqrt{r^{2}+x^{2}}\right]{/tex} dq is the charge on the ring = {tex}\sigma {/tex} area of ring {tex}=\sigma \cdot\left[\pi(r+d r)^{2}-\pi r^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}d q=\sigma \cdot \pi\left[r^{2}+d r^{2}+2 r d r-r^{2}\right]{/tex} Because dr is small, therefore, dr 2 is negligible. {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{q}=\sigma \pi(2 r d r)=2 \pi r \sigma \cdot d r{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}=\frac{ k.2 \pi r \sigma d r}{\sqrt{\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} So the potential due to charged disc {tex}\int_{0}^{V} d V=\int_{0}^{R} \frac{k. 2 \pi \sigma d r}{\sqrt{r^{2}+x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}V=k.2 \pi \sigma \cdot \int_{0}^{R} \frac{r d r}{\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{12}}=k. \pi \sigma \int_{0}^{R} r \cdot\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{-1 / 2} 2 d r{/tex} = {tex}k \pi\sigma[\sqrt{r^2+x^2}]^R_0\over {1/2}{/tex} {tex}=2 \pi k \sigma\left[\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-x\right]=\frac{2 \pi \sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-x\right]{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} R 2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} = Q(charge on disc) {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi2 R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R^{2}}[\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}-x]{/tex} thus the potential due to a disc is given by, {tex}V=\frac{2 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon _0R^{2}}[\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}-x]{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a point P on the axis perpendicular to the plane of disc and at distance x from the center O of the disc as shown in the figure.

Now consider a ring of radius r of thickness dr on a disc of radius R, as shown in the figure, Let us consider disc is divided into a large number of rings, Again let the charge on the ring is dq then potential dV due to ring at P, will be
{tex}d V=\frac{k d q}{r^{\prime}}\left[\because r^{\prime}=\sqrt{r^{2}+x^{2}}\right]{/tex}
dq is the charge on the ring = {tex}\sigma {/tex} area of ring
{tex}=\sigma \cdot\left[\pi(r+d r)^{2}-\pi r^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}d q=\sigma \cdot \pi\left[r^{2}+d r^{2}+2 r d r-r^{2}\right]{/tex}
Because dr is small, therefore, dr2 is negligible.
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{q}=\sigma \pi(2 r d r)=2 \pi r \sigma \cdot d r{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}=\frac{ k.2 \pi r \sigma d r}{\sqrt{\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)}}{/tex}
So the potential due to charged disc
{tex}\int_{0}^{V} d V=\int_{0}^{R} \frac{k. 2 \pi \sigma d r}{\sqrt{r^{2}+x^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}V=k.2 \pi \sigma \cdot \int_{0}^{R} \frac{r d r}{\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{12}}=k. \pi \sigma \int_{0}^{R} r \cdot\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{-1 / 2} 2 d r{/tex} = {tex}k \pi\sigma[\sqrt{r^2+x^2}]^R_0\over {1/2}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \pi k \sigma\left[\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-x\right]=\frac{2 \pi \sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-x\right]{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex}  {tex}\pi{/tex}R2{tex}\sigma{/tex} = Q(charge on disc) {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi2 R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R^{2}}[\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}-x]{/tex} thus the potential due to a disc is given by,
{tex}V=\frac{2 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon _0R^{2}}[\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}-x]{/tex}

question_text: Two charges -q each are separated by distance 2d. A third charge +q is kept at mid-point O. Find potential energy of +q as a function of small distance x from O due to -q charges. Sketch P.E. v/s x and convince yourself that the charge at O is in an unstable equilibrium.
question_html: Two charges -q each are separated by distance 2d. A third charge +q is kept at mid-point O. Find potential energy of +q as a function of small distance x from O due to -q charges. Sketch P.E. v/s x and convince yourself that the charge at O is in an unstable equilibrium.
solution_text: {tex}V=\frac{k q}{r}{/tex} Let equilibrium of +q is at P at a distance x from mid-point of line joining two charges. Force F A on +q is towards left side and force F B is towards right side, so for equilibrium of +q at P, F A = F B {tex}\frac{-k q^{2}}{(d-x)^{2}}=\frac{-k q^{2}}{(d+x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (d - x) 2 = (d + x) 2 d - x = d + x (Taking square root) -2x = 0 x = 0 So, Equilibrium position of charge +q between two -q charges is at mid-point (O) of line joining the two charges (-q) and (-q). Now we have to find out potential energy of +q as a function of small distance x from balance condition (O) towards any of (-q) charge. Let new position of charge (+q) from a small distance x from (O), then we have {tex}U=\frac{k \cdot(q)(-q)}{(d-x)}+\frac{k(q)(-q)}{(d+x)}\left(\because U=\frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{1}}\right){/tex} = {tex}-k q^{2}\left[\frac{1}{(d-x)}+\frac{1}{(d+x)}\right]{/tex} = {tex}-k q^{2}\left[\frac{d+x+d-x}{(d-x)(d+x)}\right]=-k q^{2}\left[\frac{2 d}{d^{2}-x^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{2 d}{\left(d^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} So, U is the P.E. as a function of x. x U 0 {tex}\frac{-2 k}{d} q^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{-2 k q^{2}}{d}\right){/tex} {tex}-\frac{d}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{-2 k q^{2}}{d}\right){/tex} +d - {tex}\alpha{/tex} d - {tex}\alpha{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}V=\frac{k q}{r}{/tex}
Let equilibrium of +q is at P at a distance x from mid-point of line joining two charges.
Force FA on +q is towards left side and force FB is towards right side, so for equilibrium of +q at P,
FA = FB
{tex}\frac{-k q^{2}}{(d-x)^{2}}=\frac{-k q^{2}}{(d+x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (d - x)2 = (d + x)2
d - x = d + x (Taking square root)
-2x = 0
x = 0
So, Equilibrium position of charge +q between two -q charges is at mid-point (O) of line joining the two charges (-q) and (-q).
Now we have to find out potential energy of +q as a function of small distance x from balance condition (O) towards any of (-q) charge.
Let new position of charge (+q) from a small distance x from (O), then we have 
{tex}U=\frac{k \cdot(q)(-q)}{(d-x)}+\frac{k(q)(-q)}{(d+x)}\left(\because U=\frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{1}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}-k q^{2}\left[\frac{1}{(d-x)}+\frac{1}{(d+x)}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}-k q^{2}\left[\frac{d+x+d-x}{(d-x)(d+x)}\right]=-k q^{2}\left[\frac{2 d}{d^{2}-x^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}U=\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{2 d}{\left(d^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}

question_text: Two charges q 1 and q 2 are placed at (0, 0, d) and (0, 0, -d) respectively. Find locus of points where the potential a zero.
question_html: Two charges q1 and q2 are placed at (0, 0, d) and (0, 0, -d) respectively. Find locus of points where the potential a zero.
solution_text: Let the potential at any point P(x, y, z) is zero then -V 1 + V 2 = 0 {tex}\frac{k q_{1}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}+\frac{k q_{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}+\frac{q_{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}+\frac{-q_{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}=\frac{-\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}^{2}}{q_{2}^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-z d}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+z d}{/tex} Componendo and dividend of {tex}\frac{x}{a}=\frac{y}{b}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x+a}{x-a}=\frac{y+b}{y-b}{/tex} Then componendo and dividend of {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}}{1}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-2 z d}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+2 d z}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}+1}{\left(\frac{q_{2}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}-1}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-2 d z+\left(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+2 d z\right)}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-2 d z-\left(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+2 d z\right)}{/tex} x 2 + y 2 + z 2 + d 2 = -2dz {tex}\left[\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}+1}{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}-1}\right]{/tex} x 2 + y 2 + z 2 = 2d {tex}\left[\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}+1}{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}-1}\right]{/tex} z + d 2 = 0 x 2 + y 2 + z 2 = 2d {tex}\left[\frac{q_{1}^{2}+q_{2}^{2}}{q_{1}^{2}-q_{2}^{2}}\right] z{/tex} + d 2 = 0 This is the equation of the sphere with centre at {tex}\left(0,0,-2d\left[\frac{q_{1}^{2}+q_{2}^{2}}{q_{1}^{2}-q_{2}^{2}}\right]\right){/tex} Note: if {tex}q_{1}=-q_{2} {/tex} then {tex}z=0{/tex} , which is a plane through mid-point. Therefore, locus of points where potential is zero is the plane through mid point of the two charges.
solution_html:
Let the potential at any point P(x, y, z) is zero then -V1 + V2 = 0
{tex}\frac{k q_{1}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}+\frac{k q_{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}+\frac{q_{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}+\frac{-q_{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}=\frac{-\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-d)^{2}}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z+d)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{q_{1}^{2}}{q_{2}^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-z d}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+z d}{/tex}

Componendo and dividend of {tex}\frac{x}{a}=\frac{y}{b}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x+a}{x-a}=\frac{y+b}{y-b}{/tex}
Then componendo and dividend of 
{tex}\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}}{1}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-2 z d}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+2 d z}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}+1}{\left(\frac{q_{2}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}-1}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-2 d z+\left(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+2 d z\right)}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}-2 d z-\left(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+d^{2}+2 d z\right)}{/tex}
x2 + y2 + z2 + d2 = -2dz{tex}\left[\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}+1}{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}-1}\right]{/tex}
x2 + y2 + z2 = 2d{tex}\left[\frac{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}+1}{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\right)^{2}-1}\right]{/tex} z + d2 = 0
x2 + y2 + z2 = 2d{tex}\left[\frac{q_{1}^{2}+q_{2}^{2}}{q_{1}^{2}-q_{2}^{2}}\right] z{/tex} + d2 = 0
This is the equation of the sphere with centre at
{tex}\left(0,0,-2d\left[\frac{q_{1}^{2}+q_{2}^{2}}{q_{1}^{2}-q_{2}^{2}}\right]\right){/tex}
Note: if {tex}q_{1}=-q_{2} {/tex} then {tex}z=0{/tex}, which is a plane through mid-point.
Therefore, locus of points where potential is zero is the plane through mid point of the two charges.
